A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.

Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed between previous and current frames
if previous_frame:
    prev_seg = previous_frame.segmentation
    curr_seg = current_frame.segmentation
    
    # Compare nodes by color and boundary string
    def node_key(n):
        return (n['color'], str(n['boundary']), n['pixels'])
    
    prev_nodes = {node_key(n): n for n in prev_seg['nodes']}
    curr_nodes = {node_key(n): n for n in curr_seg['nodes']}
    
    prev_keys = set(prev_nodes.keys())
    curr_keys = set(curr_nodes.keys())
    
    print("New nodes:", curr_keys - prev_keys)
    print("Removed nodes:", prev_keys - curr_keys)
    
    # Check blue blob
    for n in curr_seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'b':
            print(f"Blue blob: id={n['id']} pixels={n['pixels']}")
    for n in prev_seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'b':
            print(f"Prev blue blob: id={n['id']} pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Try clicking closer to the dot at [38, 33], e.g., [36, 33]
predict(expect_change=True, note='Clicking closer to dot might move it')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 33}])
print("\nResult:", last_action_result)
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Sky blue nodes after:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        print(f"  id={n['id']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
